coredata.dc:title: Rice husk assisted carbon quantum dots synthesis for amoxicillin sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Jessy Mercy, Devadass
Kiran, Venkatakrishnan
Thirumalai, Anbazhagan
Harini, Karthick
Girigoswami, Koyeli
Girigoswami, Agnishwar
coredata.dc:description: Recently, the green method synthesized biosensors have been used for numerous environmental and biomedical applications, especially nanomaterials-based biosensors. Herein, novel, environmentally friendly, and water-soluble carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been synthesized from the readily available green source (rice husk) without any ionic or covalent modifications via a one-pot synthesis method. The CQDs were characterized using UV–visible spectroscopy, SEM, XPS, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and their optical properties were determined. The stability of the synthesized CQDs was determined under the effect of pH, time, and temperature and on different excitation wavelengths. The band gap energy was calculated using Tauc’s equation with the energy of 3.2 eV, which indicated that the CQDs are fluorescent, and the linear range and association constant were determined by the Stern- Volmer relation and Bensi- Hildebrand equation, respectively. The photophysical studies indicated the strong fluorescence emission with excitation dependent behaviour and the CQDs relation with amoxicillin. Hence, in this study, the sensor producing remarkable sensitivity of antibiotics with a detection limit in the nM range was proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sensor
Amoxicillin
Green synthesis
Fluorescence spectroscopy
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots
Sensing of antibiotic residues in water
XPS and SEM image of the CQDs
Sensing of antibiotic residues in water

D.J.M. A.T. K.H. acknowledge Chettinad Academy of Research and Education for providing them with PhD fellowship.

Authors acknowledge Chettinad Hospital and Research Institute, Chettinad Academy of Research and Education for providing the research infrastructure for carrying out the work. D.J.M., A.T., K.H., acknowledge Chettinad Academy of Research and Education for providing them with PhD fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme representing the method of carbon dot synthesis using rice husk.

(A) Absorption spectrum (inset the image of the synthesized CQDs under visible and UV light), (B) FTIR spectrum, (C) Fluorescence spectra, showing the excitation and emission spectra (D) Hydrodynamic diameter, (E) Zeta potential of the synthesized CQDs.
(A) XPS survey spectrum of the synthesized CQDs, and high resolution (B) C 1 s and (C) O 1 s spectra of CQDs. (D) The FE-SEM image of the synthesized CQDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths (B) Fluorescent intensity at different pH (C) Effect of time and temperature on fluorescence intensity (D) Band gap calculation, of the synthesized CQDs.
(A) The decrease in fluorescence emission intensity of the CQDs with increasing concentration of amoxicillin, (B) Stern-Volmer plot of CQDs, (C) B-H plot of CQDs.
Comparison of LOD with the previously reported articles.

head.ce:title: Rice husk assisted carbon quantum dots synthesis for amoxicillin sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Medical Bionanotechnology, Faculty of Allied Health Sciences, Chettinad Hospital and Research Institute, Chettinad Academy of Research and Education, Chettinad Health City, Kelambakkam 603103, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chettinad Academy of Research and Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Medical Bionanotechnology, Faculty of Allied Health Sciences, Chettinad Hospital and Research Institute, Chettinad Academy of Research and Education, Chettinad Health City, Kelambakkam 603103, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, the green method synthesized biosensors have been used for numerous environmental and biomedical applications, especially nanomaterials-based biosensors. Herein, novel, environmentally friendly, and water-soluble carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been synthesized from the readily available green source (rice husk) without any ionic or covalent modifications via a one-pot synthesis method. The CQDs were characterized using UV–visible spectroscopy, SEM, XPS, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and their optical properties were determined. The stability of the synthesized CQDs was determined under the effect of pH, time, and temperature and on different excitation wavelengths. The band gap energy was calculated using Tauc’s equation with the energy of 3.2 eV, which indicated that the CQDs are fluorescent, and the linear range and association constant were determined by the Stern- Volmer relation and Bensi- Hildebrand equation, respectively. The photophysical studies indicated the strong fluorescence emission with excitation dependent behaviour and the CQDs relation with amoxicillin. Hence, in this study, the sensor producing remarkable sensitivity of antibiotics with a detection limit in the nM range was proposed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are magnificent discoveries that produced numerous treatments for various infections in a notable manner. Antibiotic residues in the environment can be a major threat to flora by interfering with their photosynthesis process resulting in ecotoxicological effects. Reports, as per Van Boeckel et al. state that antibiotic consumption in livestock in 2010 was 63,151 tons, and it will reach a peak of 67 % by the end of 2030 [1]. Exposure of antibiotics in the environment and water through the release of industries during production, supplements in feeds of livestock and aquaculture, and from human and veterinary drugs leads to the growth of antibiotic-resistant bacteria, which poses high morbidity and mortality rate. While examining the exposure period of antibiotics, many researchers found that even a trace amount of antibiotics causes long-term bacterial impressions in the environment, which alters the genomes that are responsible for mutations, protein synthesis, the biochemical process of carbohydrate and mobile genetic elements, and even causes high risk of mortality [1–3]. Low concentrations of antibiotics like ciprofloxacin, norfloxacin, tetracycline, clarithromycin, trimethoprim, ofloxacin, and erythromycin have been identified in various rivers all around the world like South Korea, Spain, China, USA, South Africa, America, Italy, Serbia, and Sweden [3,4]. Even some hazardous amount of antibiotics has also been identified in many rivers like Sulfamethoxazole and Trimethoprim. Recycling underground water into fresh drinking water and safe-guarding the contaminated sea and river water is more challenging and consumes more time [4]. Even after wastewater treatment, residual antibiotics remain in the recycled water which give rise to antibiotic resistant microorganisms [5]. Hence, it is necessary to identify even the trace level of antibiotics present in water. Presently, numerous analytical techniques have been described for identifying antibiotics. A portable miniaturized device, called biosensors, with various advantages like lower cost, quick response, high sensitivity, and high selectivity, has been used for the last few years. It is also being used for real-time monitoring of antibiotic residues [6].

The quantum dots are tiny crystals created by man which have the ability to transform a light spectrum into distinct colors. The size of each dot determines the color of the light it emits depending on its size. In order to determine the properties of quantum dots, factors such as structure, size, shape, and composition are taken into account. For various applications like sensing, imaging, electronics, medicine, photonics, and information storage, a new manufacturing technology is necessary which can produce several batches of QDs with consistent parameters for a wide range of applications [7–9]. They are particularly notable for their unique size-dependent optoelectronic capabilities, which are utilized in a variety of fields, especially in bioimaging, light-emitting diode (LED) manufacturing, photodetectors, computing, and solar cell manufacturing [10]. As the size of a semiconducting nanocrystal increases, the bandgap energy decreases, which corresponds to an increase in the wavelengths of the emission or excitation. This is indirectly proportional to the size of a semiconductor nanocrystal [11]. Due to the fact that QDs are semiconductors, they are manufactured from elements belonging to the periodic table groups II-VI (ZnO, ZnS, CdSe, CdS), III-V (GaN, GaP, InP), and IV-VI (PbSe, PbSe). The magnetic and optical characteristics of the quantum dots are frequently affected by transition metal dopants such as Mn, Cu, Fe, Cr, and Co because of their ability to influence these properties [10].

As a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials with high chemical stability and tremendous optical properties, carbon dots or carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are emerging as a promising material for studying the cell both in vitro and in vivo. Carbon dots have a wide range of optical properties that include chemiluminescence, photoluminescence, electroluminescence, near-infrared fluorescence, phosphorescence, and surface-enhanced Raman scattering. CQDs have the potential for bioimaging and sensing applications because of their properties. Pesticides, heavy metal ions, etc., can be detected using it as a fluorescent probe [12–15]. For the purpose of determining the properties of CQDs, a number of different methods of synthesis are utilized. Typically, hydrothermal synthesis is used which requires high-pressure autoclave vessels and takes a lot of time. To minimize consumption time and pressure, the one-pot synthesis method can be used instead of the hydrothermal method [16].

Our study was carried out by a one-pot synthesis method where the time taken for synthesizing the carbon quantum dots (CDQs) was less, followed by photophysical characterization of the as synthesized carbon quantum dots. To encourage green synthesis, we have utilized rice husk as the precursor for the carbon quantum dot synthesis. The fluorescence quantum yield (FQY), band gap (BG), binding efficiency, and limit of detection (LOD) calculations were done. The stability of the synthesized carbon quantum dots under the effect of different wavelength, pH, time and temperature, was performed. Finally, the sensing application for amoxicillin was carried out.

The rice husk was procured locally from Chennai, India. The hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide were purchased from MERCK and RANKEM, respectively. The quinine sulphate and sulphuric acid were purchased from HiMedia Laboratories Pvt. Ltd., India and MERCK, respectively. The amoxicillin antibiotic used for sensing was purchased from HiMedia Laboratories Pvt. Ltd., India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The rice husk was washed thoroughly with distilled water and carbonized for 4 h at 315 ͦ C. To obtain the fine powder, the carbonized rice husk was cooled down to room temperature and physically grounded with mortar and pestle. Then, in 10 ml of distilled water, 0.1 g of sample was added and ultrasonicated for 30 min. The pure sample was obtained by filtering the sample using a 0.22 µm pore size membrane filter, and the obtained solution was stored for further characterization at 4 ͦ C. The synthesized CQDs were characterized using different photophysical tools. UV–visible absorbance was measured using Shimadzu spectrophotometer to obtain the characteristic absorption peak. Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra was recorded to obtain the characteristic fluorescent peaks using Jasco spectrofluorometer (JASCO FP-8300). Dynamic light scattering was used to get the hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential was calculated for the stability of the CQDs in aqueous environment. These measurements were done using Malvern zeta sizer. FTIR (Bruker alpha FTIR spectrometer) was used to find out the characteristic bonds present in the sample which was further confirmed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis, that gave the elemental components. To obtain the surface morphology and approximate diameter of the CQDs, SEM analysis was done. To prepare the sample for SEM, the as-synthesized CQDs were coated on a grease-free and dirt-free thin glass coverslips. The coverslip was dried in a closed box to avoid dust particles and given for SEM analysis. The schematic representation of the synthesis method of carbon dots from rice husk is given in Fig. 1 .

The stability of the synthesized carbon quantum dots was determined using fluorescence intensity. The sample was excited at different excitation wavelengths to measure their stability at the emission spectrum.
ii.
Effect of pH

To study the effect of pH, 1.8 ml of distilled water was taken, and 200 μl of CQDs was added to it. The pH was adjusted with hydrochloric acid (0.1 N) from 1 to 6 and, with sodium hydroxide (0.1 N) from 7 to 13. The pH-adjusted solution was then taken to measure the emission intensities.
iii.
Effect of Time and Temperature

To monitor the effect of time and temperature, the CQDs were stored in two different temperatures (room temperature (25 °C) and 4 °C. The stability at the emission peak of both solutions was measured with a time interval of 5 days and once for 30 days.

The fluorescence quantum yield of the CQDs was determined using the quinine sulphate as reference (ϕR − 0.55), using following the equation [17,18].
ϕ S
=
ϕ R
×
E m
S ×
E m
R
E x
R
E x
S
where,
•
ϕ S denotes fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs
•
ϕ R denotes the fluorescence quantum yield of the reference
•
Em R and Em S denotes the emission intensities of reference and sample
•
Ex R and Ex S denotes the excitation intensities of reference and sample

The amount of energy required to excite an electron to the conduction band, from the valence band often denotes the band gap energy. The band gap energy of the synthesized carbon quantum dots was determined using Tauc’s plot. The precise calculation of bandgap is critical in case of determining the characteristics of semiconductors. Utilizing the optical absorption spectra, the characteristics can be determined following the literature of Tauc in 1966, and David and Mott further developed it [19].
( α h ν )
γ
= A
( h ν - E g )
where,α- The absorption coefficient.h - Planck’s constantν - Photon’s frequencyA - Proportionality constantEg – Band gap energy.γ-Nature of electronic transition
•
2 - direct allowed transitions
•
1/2 - indirectly allowed transitions
•
2/3 - direct forbidden transition
•
1/3 - indirect forbidden transition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of antibiotic residues in water
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the selective detection of amoxicillin antibiotic, to 2 ml of CQDs, different concentration of Amoxicillin solution (0.02, 0.08, 0.16, 0.31, 0.58, 1.06 and 1.45 mM) was added respectively. Then the solution was mixed thoroughly and taken to record the fluorescent emission intensities after exciting at 316 nm.

The absorption spectra of the carbon quantum dots were recorded using the UV–Visible spectrophotometer. A broad absorption spectrum was observed in the wavelength range of 280 nm – 400 nm with a characteristic peak centered at 320 nm, which indicated that the synthesized sample is carbon quantum dots. It appears that the amine and carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs are responsible for the absorption tail that extends into the visible region (Fig. 2 (A)). The CQDs were also visualized under visible light and UV light (Fig. 2(A) inset), and it was clearly seen that the CQDs were exhibiting fluorescence. Abidin et al. described in his study that, as a result of the photo induced charge separation and trapping at surface sites, there may be a radiative recombination between holes and electrons, contributing to the fluorescence emission of CQDs [20].

FTIR spectrum was recorded in order to analyze the chemical bonds present in the synthesized carbon quantum dots. Transmittance mode spectra was recorded using FTIR showed, characteristic bonds at 1413 cm−1, confirming the C-H bend and C-C stretch with the functional groups of alkanes and aromatics. The conformation peak at 3496 cm−1, 2352 cm−1, and 1637 cm−1 corresponded to the chemical bond of O-H stretch and H-bond; C≡N stretch, N-H bend, and C = O with the functional group of alcohol, phenols, nitrile, and 1° amines and carbonyl group confirmed that the synthesized sample is carbon quantum dots [21]. The wavenumber 1557 cm−1 and 1097 cm−1 indicated the functional group of aromatic and aliphatic amines with the chemical bond of C = C stretch and C-N stretch, respectively. Further, the peak at 785 cm−1 and 652 cm−1 corresponded to the chemical bond of C-Cl stretch and C-H bend, respectively, indicating the functional group of alkyl halides and aromatic rings present on the surface of the CQDs (Fig. 2(B)). A similar functional group profile was obtained by previous researchers for the CQDs synthesized in their study. FTIR signals at 3260 cm−1 corresponded O − H; C-H was indicated by a small band at 2829 cm−1, and peak at 1723 cm−1 corresponded to C = O. The peak at 1612 cm−1 confirmed the presence of C = C. The FTIR results indicated that graphitic carbon was present and there was the presence of hydroxyl group in the as-synthesized CQDs. There was no silica contamination in the sample which was confirmed by the absence of any signal at ∼400 − 1100 cm−1 [22]. The functional groups containing oxygen that are present on the CQDs surface would greatly aid their ability to be dispersed in solvents like water, ethanol, DMSO, and PBS, which could result in biological applications such as sensing, drug conjugation, and targeting moieties.

The fluorescence property of the synthesized sample was studied using spectrofluorimeter (JASCO FP-8300). The synthesized carbon quantum dots exhibited a maximum emission (λem) at 435 nm (red) when excited (λex) at 316 nm (black) (Fig. 2(C)). A red shift in the fluorescent spectrum was observed as the excitation wavelength increased from 280 nm to 400 nm, with the strongest emission spectrum occurring at 316 nm which was similar to the study of rose-heart radish executed by previous researchers [21]. From the fluorescence spectrum, it was confirmed that the synthesized CQDs exhibited fluorescence properties that can be potentially used for sensing and optical applications.

The fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs was calculated using Quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference, and the quantum yield was found to be 55.2 %. It is due to the presence of hydroxyl groups that is found at the terminal position of all carbohydrates (CQDs). Subsequently, as described by Das et al., the OH groups in primary compounds are very susceptible to oxidation, which can result in the formation of carboxyl group [9]. Therefore, this indicated that the CQDs is convenient and exhibited superior fluorescence properties.

The colloidal stability of the synthesized CQDs was determined using Zetasizer Nano ZS90 Particle Size and Zeta Potential Analyzer. The hydrodynamic diameter was found to be 220 nm for the synthesized CQDs (Fig. 2(D)). The zeta potential for the synthesized CQDs was found to be −15 mV which indicated the high stability of the synthesized CQDs in aqueous environment (Fig. 2(E)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS and SEM image of the CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the functional group present in the CQDs, we have done the XPS analysis (Fig. 3 ). The survey spectrum of XPS shows two peaks at 286 and 533 eV that corresponds to C 1 s and O 1 s, respectively (Fig. 3(A)). The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3B) was deconvoluted into three peaks at 285.4, 286.8, and 289.1 eV conforming to C − C/C = C, C = O, and O = C − O, respectively. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3(C) shows peaks at 531.4 and 534.1 eV that corresponds to C = O and O = C − O, respectively. The surface morphology was found to be spherical for the synthesized CQDs as visualized by SEM (Fig. 3(D)), with an approximate diameter of 143 nm.

The stability of the synthesized CQDs was evaluated under the effect of different excitation wavelengths, the effect of pH, and the effect of time and temperature. The fluorescence spectra showed similar emissions when excited at different wavelengths (Fig. 4 (A)). The Fig. 4(A) illustrates fluorescence spectra at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 250 to 410 nm. A noticeable shift in emission wavelengths, specifically from 435 to 458 nm, was observed in response to the variation in the excitation wavelength. This affirms the correlation between the fluorescence emission spectra and the fluorescence excitation energy, which is contingent on diverse factors. In addition to surface edge defects, these factors also include the quantum confinement effect, primarily sp2 hybridized aromatic bonds, size disparity, and the effect of zigzag edge states of CQDs.

We observed that the synthesized CQDs were highly stable at pH 7 with increasing intensity from pH 1 to 6 and with a slight decrease in intensity at pH 8 to 13 (Fig. 4(B)). The reaction mentioned in the study of Kumari et al. indicated that the CQDs that have gone through protonation and deprotonation, resulted in the variation of fluorescence intensity [23]. Further, the synthesized CQDs was stored at room temperature and 4 °C. Reading was taken for 30 days, with the interval of 5 days, once in which we observed the CQDs were high stable at 4 °C. This concluded that the synthesized CQDs were highly acceptable for other applications (Fig. 4(C)).

The band gap was calculated for the synthesized CQDs using Tauc’s relation with the help of UV– Visible spectroscopy measurements. The band gap for the CQDs was calculated as 3.2 eV using the curve on the indirect band gap by a linear fit (Fig. 4(D)). The properties of quantum dots altered the optical properties of the CQDs. This is due to the quantum confinement effects, in which the CQDs size decreased and its energy levels are divided into distinct units, leading to an increase in the band gap and a corresponding red shift in the emission spectrum [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of antibiotic residues in water
ce:section.ce:para: In order to estimate the sensing capability of the synthesized CQDs, different concentration of antibiotic was added and fluorescence intensity was noted. The stock solution concentration of 5.5 mM amoxicilin antibiotic was prepared. The fluorescence intensity was recorded for the different concentrations. We observed the fluorescence intensity was eventually decreased by the addition of different concentrations of the amoxicillin antibiotic. As the concentration of the amoxicillin increased, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with no change in the peak position (Fig. 5 (A)). This indicated the quenching of CQDs in the presence of amoxicillin even at lower concentration. In a similar way, Tian and his coworkers selectively detected the 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene residues in water with a gradual decrease in intensity [24].

The sensitivity of CQDs was determined by fluorescence intensity recorded for different concentration of amoxicillin antibiotic. Similarly, Kundu et al. estimated the quenching efficacy of RH-CQDs for various concentrations of Fe3+ based on the influence of fluorescence intensity [22]. The quenching process of the synthesized CQDs was evaluated using the Stern- Volmer plot which is expressed as,
(
F 0
/ F )
= 1 + K S V
[ Q ]

Where, F0 indicates the fluorescence intensity in the absence of antibiotic,
•
F indicates the fluorescence intensity in the presence of antibiotic.
•
Q indicates the quencher concentration
•
KSV indicates the Stern- Volmer constant.

The plot showed linearity in the concentration range of 0.02 mM to 1.45 mM with the Stern- Volmer constant of,
F 0
/ F = 1 + 1.85 ×
10
2
[ A m o x ]

The results above clearly validate the robust interaction between CQDs and amoxicillin, suggesting that the binding between amoxicillin and CQDs takes place via electrostatic intercalation. Hence, with the linearity range it was determined that the CQDs were accessible to the quencher molecule (Fig. 5(B)). Similar linearity range of Fe3+ with rice husk carbon quantum dots (RH-CQDs) were determined by Kundu et al. [22].

The stoichiometric ratio and the binding interaction of CQDs with antibiotics were determined using the Benesi-Hildebrand method [22].
1 /
(
F 0
- F )
v e r s u s
1 /
[ A m o x ]

where,
•
F0 indicates the fluorescence intensity in the absence of antibiotic
•
F indicates the fluorescence intensity in the presence of antibiotic.
The association constant was evaluated by dividing the intercept by the slope, which resulted in a straight line with a value of 36.36 M−1.

The limit of detection for the synthesized CQDs was determined by (3σ / slope) respectively where,
•
slope represents the linear line
•
3 σ represents the standard deviation of the intercept

The limit of detection was determined with the value of 1.01 × 10−4 M (Fig. 5(C)). In a previous study, a detection limit of 0.77 μM was found for Fe 3+ detection using rice husk derived CQDs [17]. Many other studies have been reported to detect the presence of amoxicillin which are tabularized in Table 1 .
Hence this concluded that the synthesized CQDs resulted in highly selective and sensitive detection probe for antibiotics.

The green route synthesis of carbon quantum dots provided a sustainable and environmentally friendly approach to the preparation. The green route utilizes renewable, non-toxic precursors and eco-friendly solvents under mild conditions. This has several properties, like a superior environmental approach, increased biocompatibility, and even large-scale production, which make them a favored synthesis route. Similarly, in this study, we have synthesized the pure carbon quantum dots via a simple method for sensing the antibiotic residues in water. The conformations of chemical bonds of the synthesized CQDs were determined by FTIR. The XPS analysis, further confirmed the elemental composition of carbon and oxygen. Moreover, the as-synthesized CQDs showed a broad absorption range of wavelength between 280 and 420 nm; the hydrodynamic diameter was recorded as 220 nm and the surface charge of the CQDs were determined as −15 mV. The shape of the CQDs were spherical with an approximate diameter of 143 nm. This indicated that the CQDs were synthesized in the nano range with high stability in aqueous environment. The fluorescence emission was recorded at 436 nm and excitation wavelength at 316 nm has been determined using fluorescence spectroscopy. The stability of the synthesized CQDs was determined under the effect of different excitation wavelengths, the effect of pH, and the effect of time and temperature and found to be stable. The band gap energy of CQDs was calculated using as Tauc’s relation and was found to be 3.2 eV. The as-synthesized particle was used for the sensing application of ß-lactum antibiotics. The linearity range and binding efficiency of CQDs with different concentrations of amoxicillin antibiotic was evaluated using the Stern-Volmer relation and the Bensi-Hildebrand equation respectively. The linearity range (KSV) was found to be = 185 M−1 and binding efficiency was calculated as 36.36 M−1, with the limit of detection 1.01 × 10−4 M. Hence, this study concluded that the synthesized CQDs exhibit a stable sensing moiety for lower concentration of ß-lactum antibiotic amoxicillin.

Devadass Jessy Mercy: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Venkatakrishnan Kiran: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Anbazhagan Thirumalai: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Karthick Harini: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Koyeli Girigoswami: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Agnishwar Girigoswami: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge Chettinad Hospital and Research Institute, Chettinad Academy of Research and Education for providing the research infrastructure for carrying out the work. D.J.M., A.T., K.H., acknowledge Chettinad Academy of Research and Education for providing them with PhD fellowship.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antimicrobials and antibiotic-resistant bacteria: a risk to the environment and to public health

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Serwecińska L. Antimicrobials and antibiotic-resistant bacteria: a risk to the environment and to public health. Water. 2020;12(12):3313.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotics in the environment: causes and consequences

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Polianciuc SI, Gurzău AE, Kiss B, Ştefan MG, Loghin F. Antibiotics in the environment: causes and consequences. Medicine and pharmacy reports. 2020;93(3):231.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotics traces in the aquatic environment: persistence and adverse environmental impact

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bilal M, Mehmood S, Rasheed T, Iqbal HM. Antibiotics traces in the aquatic environment: persistence and adverse environmental impact. Current opinion in environmental science & health. 2020;13:68-74.
Li S, Liu Y, Wu Y, Hu J, Zhang Y, Sun Q, et al. Antibiotics in global rivers. National Science Open. 2022;1(2):20220029.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Occurrence and fate of emerging pollutants in water environment and options for their removal

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Vasilachi IC, Asiminicesei DM, Fertu DI, Gavrilescu M. Occurrence and fate of emerging pollutants in water environment and options for their removal. Water. 2021;13(2):181.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in biosensors for antibiotic detection: Selectivity and signal amplification with nanomaterials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhou C, Zou H, Sun C, Li Y. Recent advances in biosensors for antibiotic detection: Selectivity and signal amplification with nanomaterials. Food chemistry. 2021;361:130109.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile Synthesis of Zinc Alloyed Cadmium Selenide (Cd/ZnSe) Quantum Dots and its Photocatalytic Activity and In Vivo Toxicity Assessment in Danio rerio Embryos

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Prakash JS, Ramachandran M, Rajamanickam K. Facile Synthesis of Zinc Alloyed Cadmium Selenide (Cd/ZnSe) Quantum Dots and its Photocatalytic Activity and In Vivo Toxicity Assessment in Danio rerio Embryos. BioNanoScience. 2021;11:437-46.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancement strategies for ZnSe based photocatalysts: application to environmental remediation and energy conversion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chauhan A, Sudhaik A, Raizada P, Khan AAP, Singh A, Van Le Q, et al. Enhancement strategies for ZnSe based photocatalysts: application to environmental remediation and energy conversion. Process Safety and Environmental Protection. 2022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Manganese oxide-carbon quantum dots nano-composites for fluorescence/magnetic resonance (T1) dual mode bioimaging, long term cell tracking, and ros scavenging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Das B, Girigoswami A, Pal P, Dhara S. Manganese oxide-carbon quantum dots nano-composites for fluorescence/magnetic resonance (T1) dual mode bioimaging, long term cell tracking, and ros scavenging. Materials Science and Engineering: C. 2019;102:427-36.
